dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 195 for an unconscious patient with rapid breathing at a residence in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Craig. I'm an EMT with EMS. Can you describe what happened before we got here?
I woke up and used the bathroom. When I came back, I found her unresponsive, about 10 minutes ago.
Did she feel unwell prior to this event or mention any symptoms before bed?
She said she felt dizzy, cold, and noticed her mouth was dry before bedtime.
We'll assess her responsiveness. Does she open her eyes or speak at all?
No, she didn't move or speak. She doesn't appear to be responding at all.
Her eye opening, verbal response, and motor function are absent. She has a GCS of 3.
Airway assessment. Her airway is patent and clear with no obstruction present.
Does she have any difficulty breathing or sounds of obstruction?
Breathing appears rapid, Kussmaul pattern. Is there any chest discomfort reported?
I've had some shortness of breath, but not severe.
Respirations are 24 per minute, slightly deep and rapid.
She has symmetric chest movement without deformity. Let me check further.
Heart rate is 81 with a regular rhythm.
Circulation assessment is normal. Skin appears pink, cool, and slightly clammy.
Blood pressure measured at 133 over 67.
No bleeding reported. Skin turgor is acceptable.
Blood glucose is 40-abnormally low.
I'll do a quick head and neck assessment. Trachea is midline.
Checking for obstruction or discharge from the nose and mouth. No abnormalities noted.
Let's look at your lungs. Chest is symmetric and atraumatic.
Breath sounds were not assessed at this time.
Pelvis intact with no instability or tenderness observed.
Back exam shows no signs of tenderness or abnormal movement.
Examining extremities and back. No signs of deformity or angulation.
Urination frequency and color were not a specific focus of concern reported.
Skin shows no specific rashes, masses observed at this time.
What medications are you currently taking?
I take metformin and glypizide for diabetes.
Have you eaten recently or taken any food close to this event?
I skipped my dinner and wasn't eating much. I have been off my regular diet.
I'll place continuous ECG monitoring now. That will help track your rhythm.
EKG shows a regular sinus rhythm. No signs of heart block or arrhythmia.
4-lead monitoring was established to further assess cardiac rhythm.
Setting up a 20-gauge IV in your left hand. We'll need it for medication.
IV placed successfully. I am ready to deliver 10% dextrose.
Administering 150 mL of dextrose to increase your blood sugar effectively.
Let me check again. Do you recognize your surroundings and current situation?
My name is Sara. I was here in my home.
Right, you're now alert but quite disoriented due to likely hypoglycemia.
SpO2 remains at 99%, stable and within normal limits.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate any pain or discomfort?
I don't feel pain. I'd rate it as 0.
Your symptoms and treatment align with diabetic hypoglycemia, protocol 3-8 in place.
Blood sugar improved after treatment and is now stable at 96.
Current pulse is 69, blood pressure 142 over 103, both improving.
Respirations steady at 22 per minute. SpO2 at 98%. All trending better.
Patient now stable with improved mental status and breathing under ETCO2 monitoring.
Let's get you into a position for safe transport. Ready to help?
We'll use a StairChair for the stairs. Let's transition you carefully to the porch.
Please stay as still as you can while we assist you during the move to the stretcher.
Your requested destination, St. Mary's Hospital, remains our choice. Still okay with that?
Yes, that's correct, I want to go there.
The patient is receiving 4 LPM oxygen via nasal cannula due to chronic reliance.
IV established, Dextrose 10% administered. Blood sugar elevated to 96 mg/dL.
Skin changed from initially cool/clammy to warm with normal turgor and appearance.
Mental status improved significantly-now alert and disoriented. No pain reported.
EKG readings consistent with stable cardiac function. Breathing is now regular and clear.
Medications include metformin and glypizide. No known allergies or adverse reactions.
Pelvis was intact, with no signs of instability or tenderness. BFAST was negative.
All actions taken per protocol for hypoglycemia. No further adverse events observed.
Patient was safely moved using a StairChair from residence to the stretcher for transport.
GCS improved from 3 to 14 after treatment. No regression in status noted.
No jugular venous distension observed during the physical examination.
Neurological findings included normocephalic head and no signs of traumatic injury.
All findings and procedures documented per protocol and verified with patient and niece.
She is now ready for care at St. Mary's. Edits and care notes have been completed.
We got you settled in a comfortable and secure position for movement.
Back was examined and is unremarkable with no abnormalities noted.
Chest is symmetric and atraumatic. No signs of trauma or injury observed.
Care has now been appropriately transferred to receiving hospital staff.
